question: Explain the difference between write-through and write-back cache policies. Which one provides better performance and why?
expectedPoints: Write-through updates both cache and main memory on every write
Write-back updates only cache initially, main memory updated on eviction
Write-back has better performance due to reduced main memory writes
Write-back requires dirty bit tracking
Write-through provides better data consistency

question: Describe the operation of a cache controller during a cache miss. What steps are involved?
expectedPoints: Check tag directory and find no match
Determine which cache line to evict (replacement policy)
If dirty, write back to main memory
Fetch required block from main memory
Load into selected cache line
Update tag, valid bit, and LRU information
Deliver data to processor

question: What is the purpose of the tag directory in a cache controller? How does it help in determining cache hits and misses?
expectedPoints: Stores address tags for each cache line
Contains upper memory address bits not used for indexing
Controller compares requested address tag with stored tags
Match with valid bit set = cache hit
No match or invalid = cache miss, needs fetch from main memory

question: Explain the concept of spatial locality and temporal locality. How does the cache controller exploit these principles?
expectedPoints: Temporal locality: recently accessed locations likely accessed again
Spatial locality: locations near recently accessed likely accessed
Cache controller loads entire blocks not single bytes
Recently accessed data kept in cache (temporal)
Block transfer captures nearby data (spatial)

question: What is a write buffer? How does it improve system performance?
expectedPoints: Write buffer is a small FIFO queue
Stores pending write operations to main memory
Processor can continue without waiting for memory write
Reduces write latency for processor
Eventually drains to main memory

question: Describe the MESI cache coherence protocol states and transitions.
expectedPoints: M (Modified): Line modified, exclusive copy, needs write-back
E (Exclusive): Clean, exclusive copy in one cache
S (Shared): Clean, may be in multiple caches
I (Invalid): Line not present in cache
Transitions occur on reads, writes, and bus requests

question: Compare direct-mapped, set-associative, and fully-associative cache mapping techniques.
expectedPoints: Direct-mapped: one location per block, simple but potential conflicts
Set-associative: set of locations (2-8 ways), balanced flexibility/complexity
Fully-associative: any location, most flexible but expensive hardware
Trade-off: flexibility vs hardware cost and speed
Hit time increases with associativity due to comparison logic

question: A cache system has a hit time of 2 cycles, miss rate of 10%, and miss penalty of 20 cycles. Calculate the Average Memory Access Time (AMAT). If we improve the cache to reduce miss rate to 5%, calculate the new AMAT and the performance improvement percentage.
expectedPoints: AMAT = Hit Time + (Miss Rate × Miss Penalty)
Original: AMAT = 2 + (0.10 × 20) = 2 + 2 = 4 cycles
New: AMAT = 2 + (0.05 × 20) = 2 + 1 = 3 cycles
Performance improvement = (Old - New) / Old × 100%
Performance improvement = (4-3)/4 × 100 = 25%

question: Explain the LRU (Least Recently Used) replacement policy in cache controllers. Describe how it works and why it is effective. Also mention one disadvantage.
expectedPoints: LRU keeps track of when each cache line was last accessed
When replacement needed, evicts line not accessed for longest time
Assumes recently used lines will be used again soon
Effective for programs with temporal locality
Disadvantage: Requires hardware to track access history (complex for high associativity)
Can be approximated with counters or stack algorithm

question: Design a cache controller state machine for handling read and write operations. Include states for idle, checking cache, handling hit, handling miss, and writing back.
expectedPoints: IDLE: Waiting for processor request
CHECK: Compare address tags, determine hit/miss
HIT: Read data from cache, update LRU, return to IDLE
MISS: Select victim line using replacement policy
WRITE_BACK: If victim dirty, write to memory
FETCH: Read block from memory into cache
WRITE_HIT: Update cache line (write-back or write-through)
Return data to processor

question: Explain the concept of cache coherence and describe how a snooping cache coherence protocol works in a multi-processor system.
expectedPoints: Cache coherence ensures all processors see same data value
Snooping: all caches monitor bus for memory transactions
On write: invalidate or update other caches copies
On read: check if other cache has modified copy
Requires bus for communication between caches
MESI protocol commonly used (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid)
Coherence traffic can limit scalability
